# dotted, `get`), and rejection of carrier / type-literal arguments.

# tokens: def m { a:5 b:7 } m.b add 1
# Two concrete maps with matching keys and unifying values satisfy
# each other when used as types.
# 9. Map value auto-evaluation — bare words must resolve
# A bare word in a map value is auto-evaluated: it must name a binding
# that resolves (a value, or a fn dispatchable with the args present).
# Bare words never degrade to data — a fn that cannot be called with
# the available args is an error. Use `/r` to store a fn as a callable
# data value, or `/q` to store a bare word as an atom.
def g fn [[] [Integer] [42]] def m {a:g} m
def base 10 def m {x:(base add 5)} m
def f fn [[x:Integer] [Integer] [add x 1]] def m {b:f} m
note: bare 1-arg fn cannot dispatch 0-arg — no silent degrade to word(f)

# tokens: def f fn [[x:Integer] [Integer] [add x 1]] def m {b:f/r} m.b 2
note: /r stores the fn as data; the arg then calls it
/q stores a bare word as atom data

# tokens: # 10. Shorthand field syntax — {foo} desugars to {foo: foo}
# A colon-less unquoted identifier is sugar for a key:value pair whose
# value is the same auto-evaluated word. The key is the base name; a
# word modifier stays on the value (/r, /q) and a trailing ? makes the
# field optional. Only unquoted identifiers trigger it.

note: /r shorthand stores the fn as data; the arg calls it
/q shorthand stores a bare word as atom data
